subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear and informative title sets context immediately
Well-defined sections with headings that guide the reader through three scenarios
Logical progression from physical description to mathematical derivation
weaknesses: Repetitive algebraic steps and duplicated definitions reduce conciseness
Long blocks of calculation could be condensed for clarity
Minor duplication of intermediate results distracts from main arguments
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the exploration has a coherent structure and clear sectioning (Intro â Scenario 1 â Scenario 2 â Scenario 3 â Reflection), its readability is hampered by unnecessary repetition and lengthy algebraic digressions. This aligns more closely with band 2 than the higher band descriptor, which requires conciseness and highly streamlined presentation.

strengths: Consistent and mostly accurate use of mathematical notation throughout
Effective use of multiple representations (labelled diagrams, algebraic derivations, Desmos graph)
Derivations and arguments are laid out with clear logical flow
weaknesses: Occasional notation slips (e.g., âusin Î¸â, missing multiplication dots)
Some very long derivative expressions could be broken into smaller steps
Inconsistent font and formatting for variables at points
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematical language, representations and logical structure are handled competently, matching the band 3 descriptor for mostly consistent use of notation, effective multi-representations, and clear communication. Minor lapses prevent placement in the top band.

strengths: Investigation is firmly grounded in the studentâs personal tennis experience
Creative extension of the basic projectile model across three realistic scenarios
Well-justified mathematical choices (e.g., Maclaurin approximation) showing initiative
weaknesses: Approach remains within standard IB/HL techniques without highly novel elements
Personal engagement is clear but not pushed to an outstandingly innovative level
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: There is significant evidence of independent thinking and a personal approach, with good creativity in model extension. However, it does not reach the exceptional, highly innovative threshold of band 3, fitting the band 2 descriptor.

strengths: Meaningful reflection on differences between models and parameter sensitivities
Thoughtful analysis of airâresistance effects and approximation limitations
Evaluation of practical implications with suggestions for future improvements
weaknesses: Reflection stops short of deep critical evaluation or exploration of error sensitivity
Limited discussion on how measurement uncertainties might alter conclusions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student provides meaningful and thoughtful analysis of results, model limitations and practical outcomes, satisfying the band 2 criteria. The work lacks the sustained and deep critical insight required for the top band.

strengths: Relevant use of projectile kinematics, trigonometric identities, differential equations and series approximations
Mathematics remains well-connected to the investigation aim throughout
Insightful limit analysis showing model behavior under extreme parameters
weaknesses: Minor algebraic errors (e.g., derivative of sin 2Î¸, Maclaurin coefficient misprint) affect precision
Some notational inaccuracies and lack of explicit discarding of invalid roots
Advanced techniques are applied effectively but not to an expert level
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration employs a wide repertoire of HL-level mathematics correctly and appropriately, demonstrating good understanding with only minor errors. This corresponds to band 3, where mathematics is commensurate with course level but not yet at the highest, expert tier.

strandSelections.reasoning: Derivations are laid out step-by-step; each scenario begins with definitions, proceeds to equations, and ends with conclusions, giving clear logical flow.  Occasional algebraic errors do not obscure the argument, so communication remains clear and well-structured.

Derivations for the first two scenarios are essentially correct; the drag model shows awareness of series approximation and domain limits, with only minor algebraic slips.  Overall understanding is good and work is mostly accurate, yet a few notational errors preclude the highest band.

The student employs algebraic equations, labelled diagrams, and a Desmos graph to illustrate results.  These different representations are used purposefully (e.g., graph to locate the optimum with drag) and complement one another, demonstrating effectiveâthough not outstandingâuse.

The report follows a clear progression: introduction and outline, three successive modelling scenarios, and a final reflection.  Each section naturally builds on the previous one (ground-level model â height model â drag model), so the reader can see how ideas develop.  Although a few algebraic digressions are lengthy, the logical pathway from physical description to mathematical derivation is maintained throughout, giving overall coherence rather than mere juxtaposition.
Introducing a three-term Maclaurin approximation, exploring limit cases, and comparing three increasingly realistic scenarios indicate creativity and initiative at a good but not exceptional level.

The discussion of air resistance lowering optimal angle, acknowledgement of approximation limits, and consideration of parameter effects shows thoughtful analysis, yet it stops short of the sustained, rigorous critique expected for the top band.

Outcomes are related to practical tennis application and the student evaluates the impact of assumptions (drag model, spin, experimental verification).  Future improvements are suggested, satisfying the descriptor for a clear evaluation.

The student reflects on why results differ between models, comments on behaviour as parameters tend to infinity, and identifies model sensitivity to u and h.  This constitutes meaningful, though not deeply critical, reflection.

Variables, trigonometric identities, derivatives and differential-equation notation are used almost consistently; minor slips (e.g., âusin Î¸â) do not seriously hinder meaning.  Equations are typeset and referenced properly, matching the band descriptor of "mostly consistent use".

The investigation is framed entirely around the studentâs tennis experience, with clear justification for each modelling choice and domain restriction; this evidences a genuine personal approach, though it is not especially novel or innovative.

Essential content dominates, but several steps are repeated (e.g., re-stating definitions or copying intermediate lines) and some long blocks of algebra could be condensed without loss.  Occasional commentary such as unit-conversion arithmetic and quotation of domain restrictions could be shortened.  Hence the work is mostly relevant yet not fully concise.

Each mathematical toolâprojectile equations, trigonometric identities, differential equations, Maclaurin seriesâis chosen because it advances the stated aim; the mathematics remains connected to maximising horizontal range in tennis.  The repertoire is wide but not fully exhaustive, aligning with ârelevant mathematics used effectively.â

Choosing a personally relevant sporting context, extending the classic projectile model, and deciding to approximate a transcendental equation with a Maclaurin series all show the studentâs own thinking beyond textbook templates.  However, these ideas remain within standard HL techniques and do not reach the âoutstandingâ level.

Sections are clearly titled, the outline matches the body, and there is a distinct reflection/works-cited section.  Figures are embedded close to the text they support.  While some derivations run across several pages and minor duplication appears, the overarching structure (Intro â Scenario 1 â Scenario 2 â Scenario 3 â Reflection) remains easy to follow, satisfying the descriptor for a well-organised exploration.

The work includes standard HL calculus, differential-equation solutions, and a power-series approximation, all solidly within HL expectations.  Although the drag section touches on material slightly beyond core HL, the depth of treatment keeps it within âfully commensurateâ rather than âexpertlyâ applied.

annotations.comment: The title is clear and effectively communicates the focus of the investigation, providing a strong opening context.
List item duplication in simplification steps distracts the reader; remove the redundant line to streamline presentation.
The introduction defines Î¸ and Rx in words but does not immediately present the symbolic form of Râ. Including the equation early would enhance clarity of key variables.
Linking the topic to personal tennis experience demonstrates genuine engagement and motivation for the investigation.
The outline neatly previews the three scenarios, giving the reader clear expectations of the reportâs structure.
The Definitions heading is appropriate, but some definitions could be moved closer to where they are first used to reduce repetition.
When solving 0 = ut sin Î¸ â Â½ gtÂ² + h, clarify the choice of root for t and explicitly state why the negative solution is discarded.
In the derivative step on page 4, dx/dÎ¸ of sin 2Î¸ was written as cos Î¸ instead of cos 2Î¸. Correcting this is essential for accurate optimization.
The notation âusin Î¸â omits a multiplication dot or space. Consistently use uÂ·sin Î¸ or uâsin Î¸ to avoid ambiguity.
The derivation concluding 45Â° is presented clearly with logical progression and appropriate algebraic steps.
The text occasionally re-states fundamental kinematics that could be condensed. Removing minor redundancies improves conciseness.
When solving the quadratic in scenario 2, explicitly state why the negative root is invalid (t < 0) to reinforce the approach.
Introducing height h in the second scenario meaningfully extends the basic model to a realistic context.
The derivative expression on page 7 is very long; consider splitting into intermediate results or summarizing using function notation for clarity.
Ensure bold and standard font formatting for variables is consistent; mixing bold u and normal u can confuse the reader.
The algebraic solution for t in the drag scenario is thorough, showing a solid grasp of advanced HL techniques.
The discussion comparing drag scenario results to previous models is insightful and demonstrates thoughtful reflection on limitations.
When rearranging for sin 2Î¸, maintain clear superscripts (uÂ²) rather than âu2â to avoid misinterpretation.
The limit analysis as uââ and hââ is insightful, showing how the model behaves under extreme parameters.
In the hââ limit step, explicitly comment on how dividing by â leads to zero in the denominator to reinforce conceptual understanding.
The percentage difference calculation is precise; adding a remark on sensitivity to measurement error would deepen the reflection.
The free-body diagram and description of drag forces provide a clear visual foundation for the linear drag model.
The separation and integration of the yâ³ equation are correct; consider spacing out each integration step for improved readability.
Introducing constants Câ and Câ correctly, but briefly restating their physical significance upon definition would reinforce comprehension.
Justification for using the first three terms of the Maclaurin series is well explained and grounded in realistic tennis shot durations.
The Maclaurin expansionâs tÂ² coefficient is cited as kÂ²/4 in the substitution but should be kÂ²/2. This misprint affects the resulting quadratic.
In the expression for x under drag, inserting a multiplication symbol between k and u sin Î¸ clarifies intended operations.
The Desmos graph effectively finds the optimum, but embedding a labeled screenshot would strengthen the multiple-representation argument.
